screens.name: Song Score Detail
screens.purpose: Present dense lead-sheet chord symbols with standard Jazz shorthand and optically balanced dedicated quality marks.

ux_rules: Preserve Finale Maestro SMuFL csymMajorSeventh and csymMinor as the approved semantic glyphs.
Keep roots, accidentals, extension numbers, and slash basses in the existing chord text font.
Evaluate quality marks by rapid recognition at the rendered mobile size, not by font-box dimensions alone.
Keep the triangle clearly smaller than the original generic geometric triangle while avoiding a weak or disappearing mark.
Keep the minor dash short while giving it enough optical weight to remain legible during score scanning.
Preserve the established raised alteration and modifier hierarchy.

screen_contexts.layout_intent: Retain the approved score density and dedicated SMuFL mapping while optically balancing each quality mark against the root and extension.
interaction_risks: Increasing both marks uniformly can make the triangle acceptable while making the minor dash too long.
Using only font-box measurements is misleading because Finale Maestro has unusually tall music-font metrics.
Excessive baseline lift can make the marks read as superscripts instead of baseline chord qualities.
notes: The current browser baseline uses 185% size and 98% baseline shift for both glyphs.
At 390px the current major glyph is about 7.11px wide and the minor glyph is about 6.00px wide.
The current dedicated marks are compact but appear slightly weak beside the bold 13.68px chord text during rapid scanning.
Baseline evidence is stored at evidence/glyph-refinement-0-baseline.png.

constraints: Complete and capture exactly three successive browser-backed adjustments after the current baseline.
Iteration 1 must test a modest shared scale increase without changing the dedicated glyph mapping.
Iteration 2 must refine shared scale and baseline alignment based on the first screenshot.
Iteration 3 may use separate major and minor optical values if the second screenshot shows their different shapes need independent treatment.
Keep each symbol in an isolated SVG tspan at regular font weight.
Do not change roots, extension numbers, raised modifiers, slash basses, score density, source song data, alphaTex generation, global theme, or dependencies.
Select the final values from the three rendered comparisons rather than accepting the last iteration automatically.
Verify mobile 390px and desktop widths with no missing glyphs or horizontal overflow.
open_decisions: Choose the final major scale and baseline shift after the three comparisons.
Choose whether the minor glyph should share the major values or use a smaller independent optical scale.

critical_judgments: Is the triangle immediately recognizable without returning to the oversized generic-character appearance?
Is the minor dash short but strong enough to survive mobile score scanning?
Do both marks read on the baseline with the root and extension number rather than as superscripts?
Does independent optical treatment, if used, remain visually consistent across major, minor, and slash-chord examples?
Do representative mobile rows retain clear chord separation and pane-width fit?
must_not_regress: non-chord SVG text and staff engraving
stored chord strings and alphaTex generation

visual_constraints: Use only the bundled Finale Maestro SMuFL quality glyphs.
Keep the final major mark visibly smaller than the former 11.84px generic triangle.
Keep the final minor mark visibly shorter than the former 8.88px mathematical minus.
Avoid any horizontal page overflow at 390px.
Final evidence must include the baseline plus all three refinements and a desktop regression screenshot.
